title: Tokyo Ghoul (2011 - 2014)
description: Tokyo Ghoul (2011 - 2014) is a japanese manga by Sui Ishida. Banned for containing violent and indecent criminal scenes.
descriptionParagraphs: Tokyo Ghoul (2011 - 2014) is a japanese manga by Sui Ishida. Banned for containing violent and indecent criminal scenes.

Its interest lies partly in the way literary or informational writing gets collapsed into a public-morality problem. As a japanese manga, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in China. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: Banned for containing violent and indecent criminal scenes. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

overview.englishDescription: Tokyo Ghoul (2011 - 2014) is a japanese manga by Sui Ishida. Banned for containing violent and indecent criminal scenes.
banningParagraphs: Tokyo Ghoul (2011 - 2014) entered censorship debates as a japanese manga associated with morality, print scandal, and sexuality. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around obscenity and public morality.

The earliest event currently captured here is 20th-21st century in China, where Chinese state censors and party authorities banned or suppressed publication. Banned for containing violent and indecent criminal scenes. Banned for containing violent and indecent criminal scenes.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: Literature Suppressed on Sexual Grounds
counterReadings.author: Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: Surveys the legal and moral language used to suppress books as obscene.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for seeing how obscenity law and censorship habits changed over time.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.
